author: fix-new-parent-slug <t@t.com>
body:
## Findings

`run_new` parsed each `--parent <handle>` with `IssueId::parse`
directly, so a slug like `agent-ergonomics` came back as
`bad_id: issue id must be 7 chars, got 16`. Other
`--parent`-taking verbs (`jjf ls`, `jjf ready`, `jjf search`)
already route through `resolve_handle(&storage, ...)`, which
short-circuits on 7-hex and walks the bookmark for slugs.

Two-line bug, modest reorder fix:

- Moved `Storage::open` ahead of `--parent` resolution so the
  resolver has a storage handle. Did NOT move it ahead of the
  body/title/slug/priority validation — those stay purely
  local so a typo'd title still fails before any IO.
- Replaced the `IssueId::parse(&raw)` call in `run_new` with
  `resolve_handle(&storage, &raw)`. Slugs work, 7-hex still
  works, and an unknown slug surfaces as `slug_not_found`
  (exit 2) the same way the read verbs report it.
- The change reordered `--parent` from "appended first" to
  "appended last" in the `Vec<DepEdge>`. Storage sorts
  `dependencies` by `(target, kind)` on every write, so
  observed read-back order is unchanged. The existing
  `new_with_parent_and_dep_compose` test sorts before
  comparing — it still passes.

## Recommendation

Land as-is. Three new integration tests in
`crates/jjf/tests/new.rs` cover the acceptance cases (slug
resolves, 7-hex still works, unknown slug exits 2 with
`slug_not_found` + the bad handle in the envelope).
`cargo nextest run --workspace` is green: 635/635.

Commit: `fbf66a8` "jjf new --parent: resolve slug or 7-hex
via resolve_handle (b417864)".

## Confidence

High. The fix is mechanical (copy the pattern from `run_ls`),
the failure mode was a single direct-parse call, and the
workspace test suite re-runs clean. The reorder is contained
to one function and the storage layer's sort guarantee covers
the only behavior the visible API exposes.

## Open follow-ups

None from this fix. The handoff doc (`b15666af`) flagged a
second `--parent` follow-up bug; that's separate work and
keeps its own ticket.
